stem: You are the general medicine registrar reviewing a 32-year-old man with two weeks of fever, sore throat, fatigue, and a faint maculopapular rash on his trunk and arms. He had unprotected receptive anal intercourse with a new male partner six weeks ago. Initial fourth-generation HIV test was negative two days into symptoms. He is now on day 14 of symptoms and feels worse rather than better.

data_table.Examination: Cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy, faint trunk and limb maculopapular rash, pharyngeal injection without exudate
data_table.Investigations: FBC: lymphopenia with platelets 110, ALT 96; EBV and CMV serology pending; rapid Strep negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Send HIV RNA viral load by PCR and repeat fourth-generation antigen/antibody combination test with infectious diseases involvement and PEP discussion review
options.rationale: Window period for fourth-generation tests is up to 4 weeks; in symptomatic likely acute HIV, HIV RNA detects earlier and confirms diagnosis with repeat serology. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat fourth-generation HIV serology in three months and provide reassurance that the initial negative result effectively excludes acute HIV infection in this patient
options.rationale: Three-month delay misses time-sensitive acute HIV diagnosis and partner notification opportunities; HIV RNA is indicated now. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral aciclovir empirically for primary Herpes simplex virus seroconversion and arrange swabs of any oral lesions detected on careful examination of the patient
options.rationale: Although HSV is possible, the rash distribution and unprotected sex history with mononucleosis-like illness mandates HIV RNA testing first. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Treat empirically for streptococcal pharyngitis with oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and arrange outpatient review by the immunology service in two to three weeks
options.rationale: Rapid Strep was negative and the pattern fits acute HIV; antibiotics will not address the diagnosis. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange a heterophile antibody (Monospot) test alone to confirm Epstein-Barr virus mononucleosis as the most likely diagnosis given the clinical presentation here today
options.rationale: EBV is in the differential but should not be tested in isolation given the high-risk exposure and need to exclude acute HIV. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion (Fiebig stages I to V) presents with mononucleosis-like illness 2 to 4 weeks after exposure: fever, lymphadenopathy, rash, sore throat, myalgia. Fourth-generation antigen/antibody combination tests detect from around 2 weeks; HIV RNA detects from around 10 days. In symptomatic patients with prior negative serology, HIV RNA is the test of choice. Early diagnosis allows ART initiation, reduces transmission risk, and supports partner notification.
key_points: Fourth-generation HIV test window 2 to 4 weeks; HIV RNA from 10 days
Acute seroconversion presents as mononucleosis-like illness 2 to 4 weeks post-exposure
Early ART initiation reduces transmission and preserves CD4
explanation.pearls: Australian U=U (undetectable equals untransmittable) messaging changed HIV care; early ART and treatment-as-prevention are central to ASHM guidance. Discuss PrEP for partners at notification.

stem: You are the general medicine registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman recently arrived from Vietnam who is being screened by the local refugee health clinic. She is asymptomatic. Her QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus is positive. Chest X-ray is normal. She is HIV negative, hepatitis B and C negative, and has no immunosuppression. She plans to start a medical degree next year and wants to know whether treatment is needed now.

data_table.Investigations: QFT-Plus positive, chest X-ray clear, sputum AFB not sent (asymptomatic), HIV negative, HBV and HCV negative, ALT 22

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her latent TB infection?

options.text: Repeat QFT-Plus in three months to confirm true positive status and exclude transient interferon-gamma release as an alternative explanation for the result obtained
options.rationale: QFT-Plus is reliable; repeat testing is not standard. Treatment should follow a single confirmed positive in appropriate clinical context. (Source: NTAC LTBI Guidelines)

options.text: Commence isoniazid monotherapy for nine months (9H) with monthly review and liver function monitoring after baseline aminotransferase establishment in this woman
options.rationale: 9H is acceptable but no longer first-line; shorter rifamycin-containing regimens have better completion and similar efficacy. (Source: NTAC LTBI Guidelines)

options.text: Defer treatment until completion of her medical degree and screen with annual QFT-Plus testing until she demonstrates evidence of active infection or symptoms suggesting reactivation
options.rationale: Untreated LTBI has 5 to 10% lifetime risk of reactivation; deferral is not justified in a young healthy person planning healthcare work. (Source: NTAC LTBI Guidelines)

options.text: Commence rifampicin and isoniazid (3HR) for three months with monthly liver function tests and counsel about hepatotoxicity and drug interactions
options.rationale: Short-course regimens (3HR daily or 3HP weekly) are equivalent to 6 to 9 months isoniazid with fewer hepatotoxicity events and better completion; first-line in current TB guidance. (Source: NTAC LTBI Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange CT chest to exclude subclinical active disease prior to commencing any latent infection treatment regimen given the high prevalence in her country of origin
options.rationale: Asymptomatic with normal chest X-ray in non-immunocompromised does not require CT; this delays treatment. (Source: NTAC LTBI Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Latent TB infection screening with QFT-Plus is offered to people from high-incidence countries, healthcare workers, immunocompromised, and contacts of active TB. Treatment is recommended in those with reactivation risk factors and reasonable expected adherence. First-line regimens are 3HR (3 months rifampicin + isoniazid), 3HP (3 months weekly rifapentine + isoniazid), or 4R (4 months rifampicin); 6H or 9H remain acceptable alternatives. Active TB must be excluded before LTBI therapy.
key_points: Exclude active TB with symptom screen and CXR before treating LTBI
Short rifamycin-containing regimens have better completion than 9H
5 to 10% lifetime reactivation risk in immunocompetent LTBI
explanation.pearls: Rifampicin interacts with combined oral contraceptive pills (reduces efficacy); counsel about alternative contraception during and for 28 days after rifamycin course.

stem: You are the geriatrics registrar reviewing a 68-year-old man who was started on levodopa eight months ago for presumed Parkinson disease. He has had limited symptomatic response, frequent backward falls, and now describes difficulty looking down at his food. His wife reports increasing speech difficulties and emotional lability. There is mild axial rigidity but limb tone is relatively preserved on examination.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 supine, 128/76 standing (no postural drop), HR 76
data_table.Examination: Limited downgaze palsy, axial rigidity greater than limb rigidity, postural instability with backward falls on pull test, hypomimia, no resting tremor, no asymmetry
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain shows midbrain atrophy with hummingbird sign; cognitive screen MoCA 22/30 with executive dysfunction

lead_in: Which feature most strongly suggests an atypical parkinsonian syndrome?

options.text: Slow shuffling gait with reduced arm swing developing gradually over four years without falls, gaze restriction, or autonomic dysfunction during follow-up assessment
options.rationale: Gradual onset shuffling gait without other red flags is typical of idiopathic PD. (Source: MDS PSP Criteria 2017)

options.text: Resting tremor with asymmetric onset and a good response to levodopa during the initial six months of treatment after the diagnosis was confirmed by movement disorders
options.rationale: These features support idiopathic Parkinson disease rather than an atypical syndrome. (Source: MDS PSP Criteria 2017)

options.text: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy with early postural instability, axial rigidity greater than limb rigidity, and poor levodopa response
options.rationale: Progressive supranuclear palsy is identified by vertical (especially downward) supranuclear gaze palsy, early postural instability with backward falls, axial-predominant rigidity, and poor levodopa response. (Source: MDS PSP Criteria 2017)

options.text: Mild bradykinesia with bilateral involvement after seven years of levodopa therapy and emergence of dyskinesias with peak-dose involuntary movements over time as therapy progresses
options.rationale: Late dyskinesias and bilateral involvement are typical PD progression rather than atypical features. (Source: MDS PSP Criteria 2017)

options.text: Mild hyposmia preceding motor symptoms by several years and REM sleep behaviour disorder with vivid dream enactment occurring during the night with bed partner
options.rationale: Hyposmia and RBD are pre-motor features of idiopathic PD, not red flags for atypical syndromes. (Source: MDS PSP Criteria 2017)

explanation.summary: Atypical parkinsonian syndromes include progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), multiple system atrophy (MSA), and corticobasal syndrome. Red flags suggesting atypical disease include early postural instability and falls (PSP), vertical gaze palsy (PSP), prominent autonomic failure (MSA), cerebellar signs (MSA-C), pyramidal signs, apraxia and alien limb (CBS), and poor levodopa response. MRI features (hummingbird sign in PSP, hot cross bun in MSA-C) support diagnosis. Survival is shorter than idiopathic PD.
key_points: Early falls + vertical gaze palsy + axial rigidity + poor levodopa = PSP
Autonomic failure + cerebellar signs + parkinsonism = MSA
Asymmetric apraxia, alien limb, cortical sensory loss = CBS
explanation.pearls: Hummingbird sign on midsagittal MRI (midbrain atrophy with preserved pons) is highly specific for PSP; MRI parkinsonism index combines midbrain and pons measurements for improved diagnostic accuracy.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 45-year-old man with five days of ascending lower limb weakness following a self-limited diarrhoeal illness two weeks ago. He now has difficulty climbing stairs and walking. He has noticed mild hand weakness today. He has no bladder or bowel changes. He is otherwise well with no chronic medical conditions.

data_table.Examination: Lower limb power 3/5 proximally, 4/5 distally, areflexia at knees and ankles, upper limb power 5/5 proximally, 4+/5 distally, sensation intact, no fasciculations
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs normal, B12 and TSH normal, MRI spine normal, CSF cell count 2, CSF protein 1.4 g/L, FVC 3.2 L (predicted 4.1 L), Negative inspiratory force minus 60 cmH2O

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 1 gram daily for five days as the standard initial management of demyelinating neuropathies in adult populations across all settings
options.rationale: Steroids are not effective in GBS (multiple trials) and are not recommended; IVIG or plasma exchange are first-line. (Source: ANZAN GBS Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 0.4 g/kg daily for five days with respiratory monitoring including four-hourly FVC and admission to a monitored bed
options.rationale: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with cytoalbuminologic dissociation needs IVIG or plasma exchange and respiratory monitoring; FVC under 20 mL/kg or under 60% predicted is an intubation trigger. (Source: ANZAN GBS Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange outpatient nerve conduction studies and review in two weeks given preserved walking and absence of any bladder or bowel involvement at this stage
options.rationale: GBS progresses rapidly and respiratory failure can develop within hours; admission is mandatory not optional. (Source: ANZAN GBS Guidelines)

options.text: Commence empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed post-infectious sepsis given recent diarrhoeal illness and arrange admission to general medical ward overnight
options.rationale: Post-Campylobacter or Zika GBS does not need antibiotics; the immune-mediated neuropathy is treated by IVIG or PLEX. (Source: ANZAN GBS Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange physiotherapy rehabilitation with discharge home as walking remains preserved and respiratory function is currently within reasonable safety margins on assessment
options.rationale: Rapid progression and FVC 78% predicted with ascending weakness requires admission and immunomodulation. (Source: ANZAN GBS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: GBS is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy classically post-Campylobacter or post-viral. CSF shows cytoalbuminologic dissociation (raised protein with normal cell count). Treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg total (usually 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) or plasma exchange; both equivalent and steroids do not help. Respiratory monitoring with serial FVC and NIF is critical; FVC under 20 mL/kg or rapidly falling triggers ICU and intubation consideration.
key_points: CSF cytoalbuminologic dissociation: high protein with normal cells
IVIG or plasma exchange equivalent; steroids ineffective
FVC under 20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than minus 30, rapid decline trigger ICU
explanation.pearls: Erasmus GBS Respiratory Insufficiency Score (EGRIS) predicts ventilation risk by combining days since onset, facial or bulbar weakness, and MRC sum score; useful for triage decisions.

stem: You are the medical registrar called to a 42-year-old woman admitted yesterday for elective cholecystectomy. She has had episodic palpitations, sweating, and headaches for six months attributed to anxiety. During induction of anaesthesia she developed sudden severe hypertension to 240/140 with arrhythmia. The surgery is now cancelled. She is in ICU. Plasma metanephrines drawn today are markedly elevated.

data_table.Vital signs: BP swings 220/130 to 90/50, HR 130 with ectopics, T 37.8, SpO2 96% on intubation
data_table.Examination: Sedated, diaphoretic, ECG shows multiple VEBs and ischaemic changes
data_table.Investigations: Plasma free metanephrines 8.4 nmol/L (normal under 0.6), normetanephrines 12.6 nmol/L (normal under 1.0), troponin rising, CT abdomen shows 4.5 cm right adrenal mass
data_table.Medications: ICU sedation; was on no antihypertensives prior to admission

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological strategy?

options.text: Commence intravenous amiodarone for the ventricular arrhythmias before any other antihypertensive therapy and arrange transfer to cardiology service for further management
options.rationale: Amiodarone does not address catecholamine excess; alpha-blockade is the foundational treatment. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous metoprolol immediately to control the tachycardia and arrhythmia before introducing alpha-blockade after the heart rate has been adequately controlled
options.rationale: Beta-blockade without prior alpha-blockade causes unopposed alpha stimulation and fatal hypertensive crisis; this is a classic catastrophic error. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guidelines)

options.text: Proceed to emergency adrenalectomy while she remains anaesthetised to definitively address the source of catecholamines without further preoperative pharmacological preparation
options.rationale: Surgery without alpha-blockade has very high mortality; 10 to 14 days of alpha-blockade with volume repletion is mandatory before resection. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous nitroprusside infusion as monotherapy to control blood pressure with titration to mean arterial pressure target without specific alpha or beta blockade
options.rationale: Nitroprusside controls BP acutely but does not block the underlying catecholamine receptor activation, leaving arrhythmia risk; structured alpha then beta-blockade is standard. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous phentolamine and titrated phenoxybenzamine, withhold any beta-blockade until alpha-blockade is established, and plan delayed surgical resection
options.rationale: Alpha-blockade must precede beta-blockade in phaeochromocytoma to avoid unopposed alpha stimulation and fatal hypertensive crisis; phenoxybenzamine is the standard agent for 10 to 14 days preoperatively. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma crisis requires alpha-blockade before beta-blockade. Phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily titrated up over 10 to 14 days is the standard preoperative agent (alternatives doxazosin or prazosin). After alpha-blockade is established, beta-blockade can be added if tachycardia persists (atenolol or metoprolol). Volume expansion with liberal salt intake is essential. Acute crisis management uses phentolamine boluses or infusion. Adrenalectomy follows 10 to 14 days of full blockade.
key_points: Alpha-blockade strictly precedes beta-blockade (10 to 14 days)
Phenoxybenzamine is the standard alpha-blocker; doxazosin or prazosin alternatives
Volume expansion is essential before surgery to prevent post-resection hypotension
explanation.pearls: Genetic counselling and testing for hereditary syndromes (SDHx, VHL, RET, NF1) is recommended in all phaeochromocytoma patients regardless of age; 30 to 40% have an identifiable germline mutation.
